treatments.name: Surgical Resection (Lobectomy) with Mediastinal Lymph Node Dissection

evidence_level.rationale: Lobectomy is the gold-standard treatment for stage I NSCLC supported by decades of phase 3 RCTs and multiple international guidelines (NCCN, ESMO). JCOG0802 and CALGB 140503 confirmed long-term survival outcomes.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year overall survival for resected stage IA NSCLC exceeds 90%. JCOG0802 reported 5-year OS of 91.1% with lobectomy and 94.3% with segmentectomy for tumors <=2 cm. Complete surgical resection provides the highest cure rate for stage I disease.

accessibility.rationale: Widely available at all major medical centers worldwide. Standard procedure covered by all insurance plans. Patient is 45 y.o. with ECOG 0 and no comorbidities, making him an ideal surgical candidate.

safety_profile.rationale: Lobectomy carries a 30-day mortality of 1-2% and morbidity of 10-15% including air leak, atrial fibrillation, and pneumonia. VATS/RATS approaches reduce morbidity. Excellent safety in fit patients like this ECOG 0 individual.

biomarker_match.rationale: Surgery is universally applicable regardless of biomarker status. Appropriate for all molecular subtypes in stage I disease. Complete resection enables molecular profiling for adjuvant therapy decisions.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical excision of the tumor-bearing lobe with systematic mediastinal lymph node dissection. Removes the primary tumor and regional lymph nodes to achieve R0 resection and accurate pathological staging.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved / Standard of Care — universally available

treatments.name: Adjuvant Osimertinib (Tagrisso) — Post-Resection

evidence_level.rationale: ADAURA phase 3 RCT (N=682) published in NEJM demonstrated statistically significant OS and DFS benefit. FDA-approved Category 1 recommendation per NCCN guidelines. Final OS analysis confirmed HR 0.49 for death in stage IB-IIIA.

survival_benefit.rationale: ADAURA showed 5-year OS of 88% vs 78% (HR 0.49, p<0.001) for the overall IB-IIIA population. For stage II-IIIA, 5-year OS was 85% vs 73%. While stage I-specific OS benefit is more modest, DFS improvement is dramatic with HR 0.17 for disease recurrence.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for adjuvant use in resected EGFR-mutant (exon 19 del or L858R) stage IB-IIIA NSCLC. Widely available oral medication. Covered by major insurance plans. 3-year treatment duration.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well tolerated with manageable side effects. Most common AEs: diarrhea (46%), paronychia (25%), dry skin (23%), stomatitis (17%). Grade 3+ AEs in approximately 20% of patients. Rare but serious ILD risk (~3%).

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets EGFR exon 19 deletion — the exact mutation this patient harbors. Exon 19 deletion is associated with superior response to EGFR TKIs compared to L858R. Third-generation TKI with high selectivity for mutant EGFR.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Third-generation, irreversible EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor that selectively targets EGFR-sensitizing mutations (exon 19 deletions and L858R) and the T790M resistance mutation, while sparing wild-type EGFR. Blocks downstream RAS-RAF-MEK and PI3K-AKT signaling cascades.
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (adjuvant, stage IB-IIIA EGFR-mutant NSCLC)

treatments.name: Osimertinib Monotherapy (First-Line for Advanced/Recurrent Disease)

evidence_level.rationale: FLAURA phase 3 RCT (N=556) published in NEJM established osimertinib as first-line standard for advanced EGFR-mutant NSCLC. FDA Category 1 approval. Median OS 38.6 months vs 31.8 months with first-generation TKIs.

survival_benefit.rationale: FLAURA: median PFS 18.9 months vs 10.2 months (HR 0.46, p<0.001). Median OS 38.6 vs 31.8 months (HR 0.80, p=0.046). Exon 19 deletion patients have even better outcomes. Relevant if disease recurs after initial surgery.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved first-line treatment for EGFR-mutant advanced NSCLC. Oral daily tablet. Global availability. Well-established insurance coverage and patient assistance programs.

safety_profile.rationale: Well tolerated with lower rate of grade 3+ AEs (34%) compared to first-generation TKIs (45%). Most common AEs: diarrhea, rash, dry skin, paronychia. ILD incidence ~4%. Superior CNS penetration reduces brain metastasis risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Specifically designed for EGFR exon 19 deletion and L858R mutations. Exon 19 deletion confers even better response than L858R. Covers T790M resistance mutation as well.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Third-generation irreversible EGFR TKI that covalently binds to C797 residue of the EGFR kinase domain. Inhibits both EGFR-sensitizing mutations and T790M resistance mutation with ~200-fold selectivity for mutant vs wild-type EGFR, reducing off-target toxicity.
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — first-line advanced EGFR-mutant NSCLC

treatments.name: Segmentectomy (Sublobar Resection) — for Peripheral T1a-b Tumors

evidence_level.rationale: JCOG0802 (N=1106) and CALGB 140503 phase 3 RCTs demonstrate non-inferior or superior OS for segmentectomy vs lobectomy in tumors <=2 cm. Published in Lancet and NEJM. NCCN Category 1 recommendation for selected patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: JCOG0802: 5-year OS 94.3% (segmentectomy) vs 91.1% (lobectomy) for peripheral tumors <=2 cm. CALGB 140503 confirmed DFS non-inferiority. Better preservation of lung function. However, higher local recurrence rate (10.5% vs 5.4%).

accessibility.rationale: Available at most thoracic surgery centers. Requires expertise in anatomical segmentectomy. Increasingly performed via VATS/robotic approaches. May be preferred if tumor is <=2 cm and peripheral.

safety_profile.rationale: Less parenchyma removed leads to better preserved lung function. Lower perioperative morbidity compared to lobectomy. Particularly advantageous for preserving pulmonary reserve. Similar 30-day mortality to lobectomy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Surgical approach is biomarker-agnostic. Applicable regardless of EGFR status. Provides tissue for complete molecular profiling to guide adjuvant therapy decisions.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Anatomical resection of the involved bronchopulmonary segment with adequate margins, preserving uninvolved segments. Includes segmental lymph node sampling. Preserves more lung parenchyma than lobectomy while maintaining oncologic adequacy for small peripheral tumors.
notable_side_effects: Higher local recurrence risk compared to lobectomy
treatments.availability: Standard of Care — available at thoracic surgery centers

treatments.name: Osimertinib + Platinum-Pemetrexed Chemotherapy (First-Line for Advanced/Recurrent)

evidence_level.rationale: FLAURA2 phase 3 RCT (N=557) published in NEJM 2025. Demonstrated significant PFS and OS improvement over osimertinib monotherapy. FDA-approved combination. NCCN Category 1 recommendation.

survival_benefit.rationale: FLAURA2: median OS 47.5 months vs 37.6 months (HR 0.77, p=0.02), a 23% reduction in death risk. Median PFS 25.5 vs 16.7 months (HR 0.62, p<0.001). For exon 19 del, PFS was 27.9 vs 19.4 months. Nearly 10-month OS improvement.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved combination. Requires IV chemotherapy infusions in addition to oral osimertinib. Cisplatin/carboplatin and pemetrexed widely available. Insurance coverage established. Relevant if disease recurs post-surgery.

safety_profile.rationale: Addition of chemotherapy increases toxicity significantly. Grade 3+ AEs more frequent than osimertinib alone. Myelosuppression, nausea, fatigue. However, manageable with supportive care. Maintenance pemetrexed adds ongoing toxicity burden.

biomarker_match.rationale: Osimertinib component directly targets EGFR exon 19 deletion. Chemotherapy component provides biomarker-independent cytotoxic coverage. Combination addresses both EGFR-driven and non-EGFR resistance pathways.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combines EGFR-targeted inhibition (osimertinib) with platinum-based DNA crosslinking (cisplatin/carboplatin) and antifolate activity (pemetrexed). The dual approach simultaneously blocks the EGFR signaling pathway and eliminates tumor cells through DNA damage, delaying emergence of resistance.
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — first-line combination for advanced EGFR-mutant NSCLC

treatments.name: Adjuvant Cisplatin-Based Chemotherapy Followed by Osimertinib

evidence_level.rationale: Sequential adjuvant chemotherapy followed by osimertinib is supported by ADAURA subgroup analyses and NCCN Category 1 recommendation. The LACE meta-analysis supports adjuvant chemotherapy for resected NSCLC (HR 0.89 for death), though benefit is modest for stage IB specifically.

survival_benefit.rationale: Combined approach: LACE meta-analysis showed 5.4% absolute OS benefit at 5 years with adjuvant chemotherapy. ADAURA showed additional survival benefit with osimertinib. Sequential strategy ensures both cytotoxic and targeted coverage, though stage IB-specific chemo benefit is debated.

accessibility.rationale: Both cisplatin-based doublets and osimertinib are FDA-approved and widely available. Standard regimens include cisplatin/pemetrexed (preferred for adenocarcinoma) or cisplatin/vinorelbine. Insurance coverage well-established.

safety_profile.rationale: Cisplatin-based chemotherapy carries significant side effects: nausea/vomiting, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, myelosuppression. Approximately 50% of patients complete planned 4 cycles. Followed by 3 years of osimertinib with its own toxicity profile. Cumulative burden is substantial.

biomarker_match.rationale: Cisplatin chemotherapy is biomarker-agnostic. Osimertinib component directly targets the patient's EGFR exon 19 deletion. The combination addresses both biomarker-driven and non-biomarker-driven recurrence mechanisms.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin creates DNA crosslinks causing apoptosis in rapidly dividing cells. Pemetrexed inhibits thymidylate synthase, DHFR, and GARFT enzymes critical for DNA synthesis. Osimertinib then provides sustained EGFR pathway suppression to eliminate residual EGFR-driven micrometastatic disease.
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R (for osimertinib component)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — both components standard of care

treatments.name: Amivantamab + Lazertinib (Rybrevant + Lazcluze)

evidence_level.rationale: MARIPOSA phase 3 RCT (N=1074) published in NEJM. Demonstrated significant PFS and OS benefit vs osimertinib. FDA-approved January 2025. NCCN Category 1 recommendation for first-line advanced EGFR-mutant NSCLC.

survival_benefit.rationale: MARIPOSA: median PFS 23.7 vs 16.6 months (HR 0.70, p<0.001). 3-year OS 60% vs 51% (HR 0.75, p=0.005). Significant benefit but head-to-head OS advantage over osimertinib+chemo not yet established. Relevant for recurrent disease.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved since January 2025. Subcutaneous formulation approved December 2025 for improved convenience. Requires biweekly IV or monthly SC administration plus daily oral lazertinib. Higher cost and access barriers than osimertinib alone.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3+ AEs in 80% of patients (vs 52% with osimertinib). Significant skin toxicity, infusion reactions (66%), venous thromboembolism (increased risk), peripheral edema, paronychia. Higher treatment discontinuation rate. Requires careful patient selection.

biomarker_match.rationale: Amivantamab is a bispecific antibody targeting both EGFR and MET receptors, addressing a common resistance pathway. Lazertinib is a third-gen EGFR TKI. Dual EGFR+MET targeting is highly relevant for EGFR exon 19 deletion.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Amivantamab is a bispecific antibody that simultaneously binds EGFR and MET receptors, blocking ligand binding, promoting receptor degradation, and engaging immune effector cells via its Fc domain. Lazertinib is a third-generation brain-penetrant EGFR TKI. The combination provides extracellular and intracellular EGFR blockade plus MET pathway inhibition.
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (January 2025) — first-line advanced EGFR-mutant NSCLC

treatments.name: Osimertinib Consolidation (Post-Chemoradiation for Unresectable Stage III)

evidence_level.rationale: LAURA phase 3 RCT demonstrated dramatic PFS benefit for consolidation osimertinib after chemoradiation in unresectable stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC. ASCO guideline recommendation. FDA-approved indication.

survival_benefit.rationale: LAURA: median PFS 39.1 vs 5.6 months (HR 0.16, p<0.001) — a profound improvement. OS data immature at initial analysis. Relevant only if patient's stage I disease is reclassified or becomes locally advanced. Dramatic PFS improvement.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for unresectable stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC after chemoradiation. Osimertinib is widely available. ASCO guideline recommendation issued 2025. Not applicable for this patient's current stage I disease.

safety_profile.rationale: Well tolerated after chemoradiation completion. Most AEs are low grade (diarrhea, dermatitis). Radiation pneumonitis must be monitored. 3% ILD/pneumonitis risk. Sequential radiation then TKI generally manageable.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets EGFR exon 19 deletion. Specifically approved for EGFR exon 19 del or L858R mutations. Excellent biomarker match for this patient's molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Following concurrent chemoradiation for unresectable stage III disease, osimertinib is administered as maintenance/consolidation therapy to suppress residual EGFR-driven microscopic disease. The combination of radiation-induced tumor damage and sustained EGFR pathway inhibition aims to prevent local and distant recurrence.
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — consolidation after CRT in stage III EGFR-mutant NSCLC

treatments.name: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple prospective studies and pooled analyses support SBRT for medically inoperable stage I NSCLC. 3-year local control rates of 90-98%. NCCN Category 2A for medically inoperable patients. Not standard for surgical candidates.

survival_benefit.rationale: 3-year OS of 82-95% in selected populations, but distant failure up to 33% at 3 years. Inferior to surgery for operable patients. 2-year OS of 97% in medically operable patients who refused surgery. Not the primary approach for this surgical candidate.

accessibility.rationale: Widely available at radiation oncology centers. Non-invasive outpatient treatment completed in 3-5 sessions. Particularly relevant as a backup option if patient becomes medically inoperable. No general anesthesia required.

safety_profile.rationale: Very well tolerated with minimal acute toxicity. Low treatment-related morbidity (10-21%). No surgical risks. Fatigue and mild chest wall pain are the most common side effects. Rare pneumonitis risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Radiation is biomarker-agnostic, effective regardless of molecular profile. Can be combined with systemic targeted therapy. Applicable across all NSCLC subtypes.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Delivers highly focused, ablative doses of radiation to the tumor in 3-5 fractions using image guidance and respiratory motion management. Causes double-strand DNA breaks and vascular damage leading to tumor cell death while minimizing exposure to surrounding normal lung tissue.

key_evidence.study_name: Pooled Analysis STARS/ROSEL and Multiple Prospective Studies

treatments.availability: FDA Approved modality — widely available, NCCN Category 2A for inoperable patients

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant Osimertinib (Perioperative Strategy)

evidence_level.rationale: NeoADAURA phase 3 RCT results presented at ASCO 2025 and published in JCO. NORA phase 2 trial (stage IA-IIIA) also showed promising results. While strong phase 3 data exists, this is not yet standard of care for stage I specifically.

survival_benefit.rationale: NeoADAURA showed major pathologic response (MPR) of 25-26% with neoadjuvant osimertinib (+/- chemo) vs 2% with chemo alone. Nodal downstaging in 53% of N2 patients. Long-term survival data (EFS, OS) still maturing.

accessibility.rationale: Not yet FDA-approved for neoadjuvant use. Available through clinical trials at major academic centers. Osimertinib is approved for other indications, enabling some off-label use. Perioperative approach gaining traction.

safety_profile.rationale: Osimertinib monotherapy well tolerated preoperatively. No significant increase in surgical complications reported. Combination with chemotherapy adds toxicity. ILD risk must be monitored before planned surgery.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets EGFR exon 19 deletion. Third-generation TKI with high selectivity. Patient's specific mutation (exon 19 del) is associated with higher response rates to osimertinib vs L858R.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Preoperative administration of osimertinib to shrink the primary tumor and eliminate occult micrometastases before surgical resection. Targets mutant EGFR signaling to induce tumor regression, potentially enabling less extensive surgery and achieving pathologic responses predictive of long-term outcomes.
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
notable_side_effects: Potential surgical delay if complications arise
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial / Emerging Standard — not yet FDA-approved for neoadjuvant use

treatments.name: Amivantamab + Chemotherapy (Post-Osimertinib Progression)

evidence_level.rationale: MARIPOSA-2 phase 3 RCT (N=657) published in Annals of Oncology. Demonstrated significant PFS improvement over chemotherapy alone. FDA-approved for second-line use after osimertinib progression.

survival_benefit.rationale: MARIPOSA-2: median PFS 6.3 vs 4.2 months (HR 0.48, p<0.001). ORR 64% vs 36%. OS trend favoring amivantamab-chemo (17.7 vs 15.3 months, HR 0.73) but did not reach prespecified significance. Modest absolute PFS benefit.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for EGFR-mutant NSCLC after osimertinib progression. Requires IV infusions. Available at major cancer centers. Relevant as a later-line option if disease recurs after adjuvant osimertinib.

safety_profile.rationale: Combination of bispecific antibody with chemotherapy leads to significant toxicity. Grade 3+ AEs common. Infusion reactions, myelosuppression, skin toxicity, VTE risk. Treatment discontinuation rates higher than chemotherapy alone.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets EGFR and MET simultaneously, addressing common osimertinib resistance mechanisms. Particularly effective when MET amplification drives resistance. Relevant for EGFR exon 19 deletion post-TKI failure.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combines amivantamab (bispecific EGFR/MET antibody) with carboplatin/pemetrexed chemotherapy. Addresses osimertinib resistance through dual receptor blockade, immune-mediated tumor killing via ADCC/ADCP, and cytotoxic chemotherapy. Targets both EGFR-dependent and EGFR-independent resistance mechanisms.
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — second-line after osimertinib progression

treatments.name: Datopotamab Deruxtecan (Dato-DXd)

evidence_level.rationale: TROPION-Lung05 phase 2 and TROPION-Lung01 phase 3 data. FDA accelerated approval June 2025 for EGFR-mutant NSCLC after prior EGFR TKI and platinum-based chemotherapy. Phase 2 evidence for the specific EGFR-mutant indication.

survival_benefit.rationale: TROPION-Lung05: confirmed ORR 43.6% in EGFR-mutant patients. Median DOR 7.0 months. TROPION-Lung01: median PFS 4.4 vs 3.7 months (HR 0.75, p=0.004). Modest PFS gains but meaningful response rates in heavily pretreated patients.

accessibility.rationale: FDA accelerated approval in June 2025. Available at cancer centers in the US. NCCN Category 2A as preferred second-line option. Priority review granted for full BLA. May become more accessible as regulatory pathway progresses.

safety_profile.rationale: TROP2-directed ADC with DXd payload. Key toxicities include stomatitis, nausea, alopecia, fatigue. ILD/pneumonitis risk (~4-5%) requires monitoring. Lower rate of severe hematologic toxicity vs chemotherapy. Manageable but requires vigilance.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets TROP2, which is broadly expressed in NSCLC regardless of EGFR status. Not directly targeting EGFR pathway but effective in EGFR-mutant tumors after TKI failure. Biomarker-agnostic mechanism provides value after targeted therapy exhaustion.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) consisting of a humanized anti-TROP2 IgG1 monoclonal antibody linked to a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (deruxtecan/DXd) via a cleavable tetrapeptide-based linker. Binds TROP2 on tumor cells, gets internalized, and releases cytotoxic payload intracellularly. Also exhibits bystander killing effect on nearby TROP2-negative cells.
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R (for FDA indication)
treatments.availability: FDA Accelerated Approval (June 2025) — third-line EGFR-mutant NSCLC

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab (Keytruda) Monotherapy — Checkpoint Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-024 and KEYNOTE-042 phase 3 RCTs established pembrolizumab for PD-L1 high NSCLC. However, EGFR-mutant patients were excluded from pivotal trials. Retrospective data shows poor response in EGFR+ tumors despite high PD-L1.

survival_benefit.rationale: Despite PD-L1 TPS >=50%, EGFR-mutant NSCLC responds poorly to checkpoint inhibitors. Median PFS only ~5.3 months in EGFR+ with high PD-L1. The immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment in EGFR+ tumors limits T-cell infiltration. Not recommended before TKI therapy.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for PD-L1 >=50% NSCLC first-line. Globally available. However, NCCN guidelines specifically recommend against immunotherapy monotherapy for EGFR-mutant NSCLC in first-line setting. May have a role post-TKI failure.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized safety profile. Immune-related AEs (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, thyroiditis) in ~15-20% of patients. Grade 3+ AEs in ~20%. Concern about increased ILD risk when used sequentially with osimertinib.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has PD-L1 TPS >=50%, but high PD-L1 in EGFR+ tumors represents constitutive activation rather than adaptive immune response. Low TMB typical in EGFR-mutant tumors. EGFR driver mutations are a negative predictive factor for checkpoint inhibitor response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1 receptor on T cells, preventing its interaction with PD-L1 and PD-L2 on tumor cells. This releases the immune checkpoint brake, enabling T-cell-mediated anti-tumor immune response. However, EGFR-mutant tumors often have an immunosuppressive microenvironment with low TMB and reduced neoantigen load.

key_evidence.journal: The Lancet / Journal of Clinical Oncology

biomarker_requirements: Not recommended as first-line for EGFR-mutant NSCLC
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — but NOT recommended first-line for EGFR-mutant NSCLC

treatments.name: Immunotherapy Post-EGFR-TKI Resistance (Checkpoint Inhibitor + Chemotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: ORIENT-31 and HARMONi-A phase 3 trials showed benefit of adding anti-PD-1/PD-L1 to chemotherapy after EGFR-TKI resistance. Growing body of evidence but not yet standard across all guidelines. EGFR+ patients were excluded from many landmark IO trials.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORIENT-31 showed PFS improvement with sintilimab + anti-VEGF + chemotherapy vs chemotherapy alone in EGFR-TKI-resistant NSCLC. Modest survival gains. Response rates improved but OS data still maturing. Best used when no targetable resistance mechanism identified.

accessibility.rationale: Checkpoint inhibitors are widely available but specific combinations for post-TKI EGFR+ patients are not universally standardized. Some regimens approved in China (sintilimab) but not FDA-approved for this specific indication. Off-label use possible.

safety_profile.rationale: Combination of immunotherapy with chemotherapy increases toxicity. Immune-related AEs on top of chemotherapy side effects. Particular concern about severe pneumonitis when used sequentially after EGFR TKI. Requires careful monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has PD-L1 >=50% which may predict some immunotherapy benefit, especially after development of TKI resistance when TMB may increase. However, baseline EGFR-mutant tumors have immunosuppressive microenvironment. Best reserved for post-TKI setting.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1/PD-L1) combined with platinum-based chemotherapy and potentially anti-VEGF therapy. After EGFR-TKI resistance develops, tumor clonal evolution may generate neoantigens and increase TMB, creating a more immunogenic tumor that responds to checkpoint blockade. Anti-VEGF normalizes tumor vasculature and enhances immune cell infiltration.
treatments.availability: Off-label or clinical trial — not FDA-approved for this specific indication

treatments.name: Patritumab Deruxtecan (HER3-DXd) — Investigational ADC

evidence_level.rationale: HERTHENA-Lung01 phase 2 and HERTHENA-Lung02 phase 3 data available. PFS improvement shown (HR 0.77, p=0.011) but failed OS endpoint. BLA voluntarily withdrawn May 2025 after OS did not improve over chemotherapy.

survival_benefit.rationale: HERTHENA-Lung02: median PFS 5.8 vs 5.4 months (HR 0.77). ORR 35.2% vs 25.3%. But median OS 16.0 vs 15.9 months (HR 0.98) — no OS benefit. Modest PFS improvement without survival translation limits clinical utility.

accessibility.rationale: BLA voluntarily withdrawn from FDA May 2025. Not commercially available. May still be accessible through clinical trials or expanded access programs. Future regulatory pathway uncertain.

safety_profile.rationale: ADC with DXd payload. Toxicities include thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, fatigue, nausea. ILD/pneumonitis risk. Grade 3+ AEs in ~40% of patients. TUXEDO-3 trial showed activity in brain metastases.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets HER3, which is broadly expressed in EGFR-mutant NSCLC. Not dependent on EGFR mutation status directly but relevant as a post-EGFR-TKI option. HER3 overexpression is common after EGFR TKI resistance.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Antibody-drug conjugate targeting HER3 (ErbB3) receptor. The anti-HER3 antibody delivers a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload (DXd) directly to HER3-expressing tumor cells. HER3 is upregulated in EGFR-mutant NSCLC, particularly after EGFR TKI resistance, making it a rational therapeutic target.
treatments.availability: Investigational — BLA withdrawn May 2025, available in clinical trials only

clinical_trials.title: NeoADAURA: Neoadjuvant Osimertinib With or Without Chemotherapy Versus Chemotherapy Alone for Resectable EGFR-Mutated NSCLC
sites: Global — multiple countries including US, Europe, Asia
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
clinical_trials.early_results: MPR rate 25-26% with neoadjuvant osimertinib (+/- chemo) vs 2% with chemo alone. Nodal downstaging 53% vs 21% for N2 patients. EFS and OS data still maturing.

clinical_trials.title: PALOMA-3: Subcutaneous Amivantamab Plus Lazertinib vs IV Amivantamab Plus Lazertinib in EGFR-Mutated Advanced NSCLC
clinical_trials.status: FDA approved December 2025 based on results
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
clinical_trials.early_results: Subcutaneous amivantamab non-inferior to IV formulation. Once-monthly SC dosing approved. Improved patient convenience and reduced infusion reactions.

clinical_trials.title: FLAURA2: Osimertinib With or Without Platinum-Pemetrexed Chemotherapy in First-Line EGFR-Mutated Advanced NSCLC
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
clinical_trials.early_results: Final OS: 47.5 vs 37.6 months (HR 0.77, p=0.02). PFS: 25.5 vs 16.7 months (HR 0.62, p<0.001). OS benefit confirmed across all prognostic subgroups including CNS metastases.

clinical_trials.title: TROPION-Lung05: Datopotamab Deruxtecan in Advanced NSCLC With Actionable Genomic Alterations
biomarker_requirements: EGFR mutation or other actionable genomic alteration
Prior EGFR-directed therapy and platinum-based chemotherapy
clinical_trials.early_results: Confirmed ORR 43.6% in EGFR-mutant patients. Median DOR 7.0 months. DCR 78.8%. Meaningful activity in heavily pretreated EGFR+ population.

clinical_trials.title: CHRYSALIS-2: Amivantamab Plus Lazertinib in Atypical EGFR-Mutated and Post-Osimertinib NSCLC
clinical_trials.early_results: Cohort A (post-osimertinib + chemo): ORR 36% with amivantamab + lazertinib. Durable responses observed. Activity seen across various resistance mechanisms.

clinical_trials.title: NORA: Neoadjuvant and Adjuvant Osimertinib in Stage IA to IIIA EGFR-Mutant NSCLC
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
Stage IA to IIIA resectable NSCLC
clinical_trials.early_results: Perioperative osimertinib showed feasibility and encouraging pathologic responses. No surgical safety concerns. Supports perioperative targeted therapy approach for EGFR+ early-stage NSCLC.

clinical_trials.title: LAURA: Osimertinib Consolidation After Chemoradiation in Unresectable Stage III EGFR-Mutant NSCLC
biomarker_requirements: EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation
clinical_trials.early_results: Median PFS 39.1 vs 5.6 months (HR 0.16, p<0.001). Unprecedented PFS benefit. OS data immature but favorable trend.

combination_strategies.rationale: Complete surgical resection followed by adjuvant osimertinib addresses both macroscopic disease removal and EGFR-driven micrometastatic residual disease. ADAURA demonstrated 5-year OS of 88% vs 78% (HR 0.49) in the overall stage IB-IIIA population. The standard perioperative approach for resected EGFR-mutant NSCLC.

Perioperative strategy combining neoadjuvant osimertinib (+/- chemo) with surgery and adjuvant osimertinib. NeoADAURA showed MPR of 25-26% with neoadjuvant osimertinib vs 2% with chemo alone. Nodal downstaging in 53% of N2 patients. Addresses tumor early while enabling pathologic response assessment.

For advanced/recurrent disease: combining EGFR TKI with cytotoxic chemotherapy delays resistance by attacking both EGFR-driven and non-EGFR-driven tumor populations simultaneously. FLAURA2 showed OS improvement of nearly 10 months (47.5 vs 37.6 months, HR 0.77). Addresses tumor heterogeneity.

Dual targeting of EGFR (extracellular + intracellular) plus MET receptor inhibition. Addresses MET amplification — one of the most common osimertinib resistance mechanisms. MARIPOSA: 3-year OS 60% vs 51% (HR 0.75). Proactively blocks major resistance pathways.

Post-osimertinib resistance strategy. Bispecific EGFR/MET antibody combined with cytotoxic chemotherapy attacks resistant tumors through multiple mechanisms including receptor degradation, immune activation (ADCC), and DNA damage. PFS 6.3 vs 4.2 months (HR 0.48). Effective regardless of specific resistance mechanism.

Oligoprogression management strategy: SBRT to progressing sites while continuing systemic EGFR TKI therapy. Delays switch to next-line therapy. Prospective data from stage IV studies showed delayed acquired resistance and prolonged PFS and OS. Applicable for limited recurrence after curative-intent treatment.

supportive_care.evidence: Multiple systematic reviews and meta-analyses (2023-2025) demonstrate that both prehabilitation and postoperative pulmonary rehabilitation improve functional capacity, pulmonary function, and quality of life in NSCLC surgical patients. Supervised physiologically targeted programs showed the greatest benefit.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced postoperative pulmonary complications, accelerated recovery of exercise capacity, improved respiratory symptoms, and enhanced health-related quality of life. Preoperative rehabilitation reliably improved presurgical fitness metrics.

supportive_care.evidence: Strong evidence that continued smoking after lung cancer diagnosis is associated with worse survival, increased treatment toxicity, higher recurrence rates, and increased risk of second primary cancers. NCCN and ASCO guidelines recommend cessation for all lung cancer patients.
supportive_care.benefit: Improved surgical outcomes, reduced perioperative complications, enhanced response to systemic therapy, reduced risk of second primary malignancies, and improved overall survival. The patient has smoking history making this intervention essential.

supportive_care.evidence: Systematic reviews show that multimodal prehabilitation including nutritional supplementation improves postoperative outcomes. Adequate protein intake, immunonutrition (arginine, omega-3, nucleotides), and correction of nutritional deficiencies are supported by perioperative care guidelines.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced surgical complications, faster wound healing, improved immune function, better tolerance of adjuvant therapy, and maintenance of lean body mass during treatment. Particularly important during cisplatin-based chemotherapy which can cause nausea and appetite loss.

supportive_care.approach: Psychological Support and Cognitive Behavioral Therapy
supportive_care.evidence: Meta-analyses demonstrate that psychological interventions improve quality of life, reduce anxiety and depression, and may improve adherence to treatment in cancer patients. Mindfulness-based stress reduction and cognitive behavioral therapy have the strongest evidence base.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced psychological distress, improved treatment adherence, better quality of life during adjuvant therapy, and enhanced coping mechanisms. Young patients (45 y.o.) may face unique psychological challenges regarding cancer diagnosis.

supportive_care.evidence: Prospective studies show that personalized home physical activity improves postoperative quality of life and cancer biomarkers in NSCLC patients. ASCO Clinical Practice Guidelines recommend regular physical activity during and after cancer treatment.
supportive_care.benefit: Improved cardiopulmonary fitness, reduced fatigue, enhanced immune function, lower recurrence risk, and better overall survival. Exercise programs are feasible and safe throughout perioperative phases with consistent improvements in functional capacity.

supportive_care.evidence: ADAURA MRD substudy (Nature Medicine, 2025) demonstrated that circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) clearance during adjuvant osimertinib predicts DFS benefit. MRD-based monitoring is increasingly integrated into clinical decision-making for resected NSCLC.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of recurrence before radiographic progression, potential to guide treatment duration decisions (adjuvant therapy de-escalation or intensification), and identification of emerging resistance mechanisms through genomic profiling of ctDNA.
